title: The secret behind planet-warming burps
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Newton’s law of gravity passes its biggest test ever, extra-thick kangaroo enamel, and more
Brought to you by Danaher Corporation
View this email in your browser
4 May 2026
Today’s What We’re Enjoying features a review from Science Managing Photography Editor Emily Petersen. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including a big test for a famous law and kangaroos’ tough teeth.
Microbiology  |  Science
The secret behind planet-warming burps
Drinking fizzy liquids and eating too quickly can bring out the belch in anyone. But what if those burps weren’t just a tad embarrassing, but actively heating up the planet?

Cows and other ruminant animals belch copious amounts of methane, a potent greenhouse gas. Now, researchers finally know just what makes their burps so methane-rich: newly discovered organelles called hydrogenobodies. They live within single-celled protozoa called ciliates, that themselves live in the first stomach of cows called the rumen, where plant matter gets broken down.

To find the methane menaces, researchers analyzed the genomes of 450 ciliates living in rumens, most of which had never been sequenced. They noticed that dairy cows with more ciliates produced more methane. The reason came down to a new organelle they dubbed the hydrogenobody, which produces hydrogen—a process that stimulates other microbes to produce methane. The finding is a “ mechanistic breakthrough ,” animal scientist Ermias Kebreab told Scientific American.

The researchers hope the discovery could lead to new angles for controlling ruminants’ methane emissions. While previous efforts have found that removing ciliates does reduce cows’ methane emissions, it also reduced the amount of milk they produced.
Read the paper
Astrophysics  |  News from Science
Newton’s law of gravity passes its biggest test ever
The galaxy cluster Abell-370 contains hundreds of galaxies. Researchers probed how gravity between such giant structures varied across hundreds of millions of light-years.  J. Lotz and the HFF Team/STSci/NASA/ESA
More than three centuries after Isaac Newton proposed his law of gravity, cosmologists have confirmed it using the largest objects in the universe. Newton’s famous inverse square law had long been verified in laboratory experiments and within the Solar System. The new study extends the law to the largest scales possible: galaxy clusters separated by hundreds of millions of light-years.

Newton’s law says that the gravitational force between two massive objects varies inversely with the square of the distance between them. Published in 1687, the formula immediately enabled Newton to explain the orbits of the planets. A century later, Henry Cavendish confirmed the law in the lab, by suspending a small dumbbell from a fine wire.

Now, researchers with the Atacama Cosmology Telescope have probed the force law across hundreds of thousands of clusters by combining separate statistical measures of their positions and the speeds with which they move. Just as planets closer to the Sun move faster, two clusters that are closer together will move faster relative to each other, explained co-author and cosmologist Kris Pardo. So, measuring how the relative speed of any two clusters varies with the distance between them effectively probes the nature of gravity; the subtle statistical analysis confirmed Newton’s law.
Read the full story
Paleontology  |  Science
Grass is tough, so kangaroos got tougher
Grasses are tough to chew, often coated in dust, and packed with microscopic silica particles that grind teeth down. That’s why herbivorous animals whose diets depend primarily on grass—like horses, deer, and other hoofed animals—have evolved chompers that are up for the challenge. Their teeth are tall, with thin enamel, built for grinding the plants side to side. Meanwhile, early kangaroos and their relatives fed on softer foods like leaves and fruit, so their teeth were shorter, adapted for more vertical chewing.

New research in Science shows that when grasses became more common across Australia during the Miocene (roughly 20 to 10 million years ago), kangaroos reinforced their shorter teeth with thicker enamel . Using high-resolution scans of fossil and modern teeth, researchers measured enamel density across different parts of each animal’s molars, including the ridges and basins where chewing stress is highest, and compared these patterns across 76 fossil and living species over time. They found that kangaroos evolved thick enamel, allowing them to withstand the wear of abrasive diets, unlike other herbivores, who changed the shape of their teeth entirely.

And this case of convergent evolution didn’t put them at a disadvantage, as kangaroos came to dominate Australia’s grazing mammals, largely replacing marsupials with more ungulatelike, side-to-side chewing teeth, leaving wombats and koalas as the last holdouts.
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Danaher combines AI-driven discovery with a proven strategic framework to expand what’s possible for the future of healthcare.
Read Now
What we’re Enjoying
The Dark Frontier
Jeffrey Marlow
Some would argue that the deep sea, not space, is truly the final frontier. After all, we have better maps of Mars than we do of the sea floor. That means there’s a lot left to learn about the darkest depths of our own world—and a lot we could destroy in the process. In The Dark Frontier, marine microbiologist Jeffrey Marlow traces the history of deep-sea research from its founding in the late 19th century to underwater missions today. “Marlow brings the old saws of scientific lore back to life with fresh details and an eye for the bizarre,” writes reviewer Laura Trethewey. In particular, he highlights how, just like with other frontiers, exploration and exploitation are often intertwined. “Ultimately, The Dark Frontier revels in the fun and fervor of chasing the unknown, while also reminding readers that every discovery has a dark side,” says Trethewey.
Read the full review
True Color
Kory Stamper
Color—even more so than beauty—really is in the eye of the beholder (as anyone who has played Hues and Cues knows all too well). But someone had to describe the difference between fuchsia, magenta, and hot pink for the dictionary. In True Color, Kory Stamper tells their stories—those of the lexicographers and specialist consultants who decided upon the specific definitions for colors for Merriam-Webster. “What ultimately makes it work,” reviewer (and Science Contributing Correspondent) Kai Kupferschmidt writes, “is Stamper’s voice, her humor, the precision of her prose, and her determination—palpable on every page—to give words and those who live their lives in service of them their due.”
Read the full review
Life Unearthed with Ariel Waldman
PBS
Life Unearthed with Ariel Waldman is a new science-driven docu-series on PBS. I had the privilege to screen a few of the episodes recently and hear from the creator. With a degree in graphic design and experience working for NASA, Waldman has a unique background and curious perspective of our natural world. In her debut TV series, she isn’t afraid to play with scale and show us the power and influence that tiny, microscopic worlds have all over the planet, from Antarctica to the U.S. prairies. As a science-minded photographer, she captures the personalities and drama in these microecosystems, and yes—tardigrades very much star in the show. Be sure to check it out!
—Emily Petersen, Managing Photography Editor, Science
Filmmaker Ariel Waldman (right) and science-cakemaker Rose McAdoo pose behind a 3-foot-long tardigrade cake that was on display at Waldman’s film premiere of Life Unearthed at the American Museum of Natural History. The new docu-series is releasing weekly on PBS.  Michael Bradshaw
See more at PBS
Be sure to check out all the reviews in our Books et al. section.
Et Cetera
Revealing a lost world
Along the eastern front of the Appalachian Mountains, buried just below the surface, lies a fragment of a lost continent. Known as the Piedmont Resistor, this piece of Pangaea is one of the signature discoveries of the Magnetotelluric Array in its two decades of operation. “You can see all the sutures,” said one of the scientists. “And those boundaries, even though they’re a billion years old, are where you look for mineral resources.”
Reviews of Geophysics Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
Not so modern family
DNA from hundreds of skeletons reveals that for generations leading up to Rome’s collapse, migrants from the north trickled into Roman frontier provinces and intermarried with Romans, and in many cases adopted their customs. The findings upend the long-held notion that these migrants killed or displaced the Romans and helped spell the empire’s doom. “We see people dropping in in small groups, attracted by Rome when it still exists,” explained one of the scientists.
Nature Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
A menstruation model
Endometrial organoids can now regenerate much like the uterine lining does after menstruation. “It is fantastic to have a model system that you can do experiments on,” said one evolutionary biologist, especially given studying the process in people is too invasive, and common lab organisms do not naturally menstruate like humans do.
Cell Stem Cell Paper  |  Read more at Nature
Epigenetic findings must not be weaponized to suggest that disability is simply a product of lifestyle choices or parental failures, nor to fuel deterministic or reductive narratives about the impact of individual behaviors on the health of future generations.
Expert Voices  |  23 April 2026  |  Jackie Leach Scully
Last but not least
After all the decades that scientists have studied mice, it’s wild that there’s still paradigm-shifting information to discover about the animals’ biology—like that their smell receptor cells are distinctly organized. (Side note: The image of fluorescently labeled nasal tissue looks like it belongs in a modern art gallery; I would totally hang that on my wall.)

And to all you Star Wars fans out there, May the 4th be with you!
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Hannah Richter, Adrian Cho, and Ana Georgescu

items.title: a new organelle they dubbed the hydrogenobody
extends the law to the largest scales possible
kangaroos reinforced their shorter teeth with thicker enamel
like that their smell receptor cells are distinctly organized
